Employee Communications Plan
Pre-Close Through Year 1 Messaging Framework
Company
{{companyName}}
Prepared For
{{recipientName}}
Date
{{sentDate}}
Reference
{{ref}}
§ 01Communication Principles

Every employee communication during the ESOP transition should follow these core principles:

A.Transparency without speculation. Share what is known, confirm what is decided, and clearly label what is still being determined. Employees can handle uncertainty better than silence.
B.Answer "what does this mean for me?" Every message should address the employee's personal impact. Ownership is abstract until it connects to their paycheck, retirement, and daily work.
C.No promises about stock value. ESOP account values fluctuate. Never imply a specific return, growth rate, or payout amount. Use ranges and qualify all projections.
D.Consistent messaging across all channels. The same facts must be shared in town halls, written announcements, one-on-one conversations, and informal settings. Mixed messages erode trust.
E.Repeat, repeat, repeat. Employees need to hear the same core messages multiple times before they internalize them. Plan for redundancy.
Guiding question: If an employee reads only this one message, would they understand what is happening, why, and what it means for them personally?
§ 02Pre-Close (Confidential Phase)

Before closing, the transaction is confidential. Only the owner and designated leadership should know. The goal is alignment — not announcement.

Leadership alignment meetings (recommended):

1.Owner + senior leadership. Share the ESOP decision, explain the why, address concerns, align on messaging. Schedule 2–4 weeks before close.
2.Mid-level managers. Brief managers on what they can and cannot say. Provide a rumor-control script. Schedule 1–2 weeks before close.
3.HR / payroll. Ensure HR understands the mechanics — no changes to pay, benefits, or job descriptions on Day 1. Employees will ask HR first.

If employees ask before the announcement:

Rumor-control script: "I appreciate you asking. We're always looking at ways to strengthen the company and take care of our team. When there's something concrete to share, everyone will hear it at the same time. Nothing is changing today."
§ 03Announcement Day

The announcement should happen in a single, company-wide event. All employees hear the same message at the same time.

Recommended all-hands agenda (30–45 minutes):

1.Opening (5 min). Owner shares the personal why — legacy, employees, community. This is the emotional anchor.
2.What is an ESOP? (10 min). Plain-language explanation. Use analogies: "Think of it like a retirement account that grows as the company grows — and it costs you nothing."
3.What changes — and what doesn't (10 min). Same name, same management, same jobs. The difference is that employees now have a beneficial ownership stake.
4.Q&A (15–20 min). Prepare for these top questions: Do I get stock? Can I sell it? What if I leave? Does this affect my pay? When do I see my account?
Key message: "Today, {{companyName}} becomes employee-owned. Your job is the same. Your benefits are the same. The difference is that you now have a financial stake in the company's success — at no cost to you."
§ 04First 90 Days

After the announcement, employees need time to absorb. Plan one communication per month for the first three months.

A.Month 1 — "What is an ESOP?" Written FAQ distributed to all employees. Covers: what it is, how it works, who is eligible, what happens to my existing retirement benefits. Plain language, no jargon.
B.Month 2 — "How does it work for me?" Covers: vesting schedule, how account value is determined, when you receive statements, what happens if you leave or retire. Include a timeline graphic.
C.Month 3 — "What are my rights?" Covers: voting rights, information rights, diversification options (after age 55 with 10+ years), distribution timing. Reference the Summary Plan Description.
Tip: Deliver each communication in two formats — a written handout or email, and a brief all-hands or team meeting where employees can ask questions.
§ 05Ongoing Communications

ESOP communication is not a one-time event. Build these into the company's recurring rhythm:

A.Quarterly updates. Brief company performance summary tied to ESOP value. Revenue, key wins, challenges. Keep it simple and honest.
B.Annual ESOP meeting. Required by law for plans with 100+ participants. Review the year, present account statements, explain the valuation, recognize employee contributions.
C.Distribution and vesting communications. Personalized letters when employees vest, become eligible for diversification, or receive distributions. Timing and tax implications must be clear.
D.ESOP committee. Form an employee-led ESOP committee to serve as a communication bridge. Committee members attend training, relay questions from peers, and help plan events.
Best practice: Track the questions employees ask. Recurring questions reveal gaps in communication. Address the top three questions in each quarterly update.
§ 06Messaging Do's and Don'ts
Do
✓Use plain language — "ownership stake," not "beneficial interest in the trust"
✓Acknowledge uncertainty honestly — "We don't know the exact value yet"
✓Celebrate milestones — anniversary, first statement, first distribution
✓Connect ownership to daily work — "Your effort builds the value"
✓Provide multiple channels — town halls, written docs, one-on-ones, email
✓Invite questions — "There are no bad questions about your ownership"
Don't
✗Promise specific account values or growth rates
✗Use jargon — DOL, ERISA, QRP, vesting cliff (without explanation)
✗Compare to publicly traded stock or the stock market
✗Imply employees can sell their shares freely
✗Dismiss concerns — "Don't worry about it" erodes trust
✗Communicate once and assume the message landed
—Forhemit Transition Stewardship
Forhemit Transition Stewardship
California Public Benefit Corporation
